Off Calendar Jun-23-2023 Continued To Jan-17-24 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

STATUS CONFERENCE TO SCHEDULE "FURTHER PROCEEDINGS" (ADDED PER ORDER FILED 3/17/23)
Off Calendar Jun-06-2023 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances (Via Zoom):

Norman LaForce, Esq., Law Offices of, (510) 295-7657, for Plaintiff.

Cheryl Schreck, Esq., Fisher & Phillips, LLP, (213) 330-4500, for Defendant Rainberry, Inc./Specially Appearing for Defendant Trinet, HR, III, Inc.

Ruling - A R G U E D. Upon conclusion of argument, the Court takes the subject matter under submission. In employment litigation, this court granted a motion to compel arbitration on October 24, 2022. Procedural unconscionability was found in the arbitration agreement, but not substantive, and both forms are required. (Armendariz v. Foundation Health Psychcare Services, Inc. (2000) 24 Cal.4th 83, 114.) The October 2022 order rejected three substantive unconscionability arguments, based on confidentiality provisions, a JAMS fee and failure to attach JAMS rules to the arbitration agreement. Plaintiff sought writ review. On March 16, 2023, after "detailed review of the entire record," the court of appeal issued an order disagreeing with this court on the first issue, citing a "comparable" confidentiality provision in Ramos v. Sup. Ct. (2018) 28 Cal.App.5th 1042. The court of appeal left the October 22 order's other substantive unconscionability findings undisturbed, along with this court's finding that the arbitration agreement was procedurally unconscionable due in part to plaintiff's health concerns. The appellate court commanded this court to "reconsider whether to compel the parties to arbitration," stating that "courts have discretion to sever or limit the application of unconscionable provisions." Reconsideration is based on new law or facts (CCP 1008), here the court of appeal's finding that the arbitration agreement's confidentiality provisions were substantively unconscionable under Ramos. As suggested by the court of appeal, this court received post-writ briefing from the parties. Defendants say the confidentiality provisions should be severed and stricken and arbitration should proceed, because those provisions were merely collateral and not directed to the arbitration agreement's central purpose of efficient dispute resolution. (See Armendariz, 24 Cal.4th at 124.) This is consistent with California courts' "very liberal view of severability" (Adair v. Stockton Unified School District (2008) 162 Cal.App.5th 1436, 1450) and the "strong policy favoring arbitration" (Ramos, 28 Cal.App.5th at 1051 ["Any doubts concerning the scope of arbitral issues will be resolved in favor of arbitration."].) Plaintiff's lead argument is that Ramos, with its comparable confidentiality provisions, was not compelled to arbitration, so this case should not be either. However, Ramos had several types of substantively unconscionable provisions, while our case (per the court of appeal's "detailed review of the entire record") had one (confidentiality). (Id. at 1064-65.) Anticipating this point, plaintiff argues old substantive unconscionability facts that were either raised in 2022 or should have been. Upon reconsideration, this court exercises its discretion to sever and strike the arbitration agreement's confidentiality provisions and again orders the case to arbitration, staying it in this court.

Counsel Cheryl Schreck is required to prepare a proposed form of order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling.

Judge: Richard B, Ulmer Jr., Clerk: R. Woods, Court Reporter: Peggy Tsujimoto. CSR: 5229. T: (415) 828-1919. E: peggytsujimoto@gmail.com. (302/RBU)
